data.title: W. P. Carey Is A Buy Under This Condition
data.description:
For Clarity

The last time I covered W. P. Carey (NYSE:WPC), I declared that I wasn't adding anything to my stake. For clarity, that hasn't changed. I explained that even though the decision to exit office properties was good, I considered the communication around it bad; therefore, I preferred (and still do) to allocate elsewhere.

However, I've seen many comments and received some requests to cover WPC abstracting from the 'communication factor' and concentrate solely on the business merit and valuation aspects.

With that in mind, I decided to provide an objective overview of WPC without considering the communication around the decision to exit the office property sector.

For transparency, as I indicated in my previous coverage, I haven't sold my position nor added to it. Personally, WPC still remains a 'hold' to me, but let's give it a try on the abovementioned assumption. Should you be willing to better grasp my complete, more subjective take on the Company, please refer to the link in this section's first sentence.

Without further ado - let's review WPC!

The Best Thing About WPC - Valuation Outlook

As an M&A advisor, I usually rely on the multiple valuation method, a leading tool in transactional processes. This method allows for accessible and market-driven benchmarking. Utilizing so-called 'multiples' facilitates determining whether a given stock price is justified, as these multiples combine a given 'value' metric and a given 'financial results' metric.

It still amazes me how many investors take solely the stock price into account. This may lead to misjudgments for several reasons, such as changes in the number of shares outstanding (due to share repurchases or issues) or a change in a business's scale. That's why multiples are constructed the way they are—by referring to a given 'financial' metric.

As we discuss a REIT here, I will use a P/FFO multiple to provide a perspective on WPC's valuation. The forward-looking P/FFO multiple stood at:

  • 12.7x for WPC
  • 14.7x for Realty Income (O)
  • 18.2x for Agree Realty (ADC)
  • 17.4x for Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT)
  • 14.4x for NNN REIT (NNN)
  • 12.3x for Broadstone Net Lease (BNL)

That's a relatively large reference group, but I decided it's worth presenting valuation multiples of players from different property sectors. WPC is a diversified REIT leaning towards the industrial property sector, with still meaningful exposure to retail properties. The factor ensuring WPC's comparability to the above REITs is its focus on triple-net leases.

The conclusion is simple - WPC is discounted from its historical and market perspectives. Moreover, the company hasn't fully benefited from the improving investors' sentiment towards the REIT sector. It seems that investors still punish WPC for the communication controversy, but as promised - we will not get into that and determine WPC's upside potential regardless of this matter (in the latter sections).

W. P. Carey - Much Improved Quality

Overview

The Company is a diversified REIT with 1291 properties (excl. self-storage) comprising 170.1m sq. ft. located across different regions, including the US, Canada, Europe, Mexico, etc.

Its portfolio heavily leans towards warehouse/industrial properties (64% of ABR), supplemented by another relatively substantial category - retail properties (21% of ABR).

Therefore, WPC has earned its 'diversified' characteristic through tenant diversification (346 tenants, with the Top 10 responsible for just 19.5% of ABR), geography, tenants' industry, and property sector.

While the market tends to value diversified REITs with a discount compared to specialized ones, WPC stands out with its top-tier business metrics.

One can't deny WPC's quality, as it is well-reflected within its business metrics

Before we delve into WPC's Q2 2024 results, let's emphasize its property exposure, as it's crucial for interpreting its metrics - 64% of WPC's ABR is derived from industrial properties.

For context, the industrial property sector has been facing some headwinds since ~mid-2022. Outstandingly high net completions observed during the 2022-2023 period, combined with the high-interest rate environment, drove the vacancy rate to a high 6.5% level, as indicated in the Wells Fargo report.

As a result, many sector representatives have been struggling with decreasing occupancy due to the negative supply-to-demand relationship. Please review the table below for the occupancy rate levels across industrial players.

As we can see, WPC recorded the highest occupancy rate across the chosen entities. However, one has to keep in mind that WPC is not directly comparable to other industrial REITs due to its heavy focus on triple net leases and mission-critical properties.

Naturally, occupancy was also driven by its properties from other sectors (e.g. retail). So... let's review the same table for some retail/service-oriented REITs.

Conclusion - yes, most retail-oriented players recorded a higher occupancy rate, but given its overwhelming exposure to industrial properties, I consider WPC's occupancy solid.

Moreover, WPC has an outstanding (~12 years) weighted average lease term (WALT), reflecting its negotiating strength and portfolio quality. Its 12-year WALT places it significantly above industrial players and within the top level of retail/service-oriented players.

It's also important to realize that the market rents of industrial properties have grown more dynamically than the rents subject to contractual rent escalators, which typically amount to low single digits. As a result, WPC can bump its rents noticeably as the leases expire, which will be a positive factor for its AFFO. Nevertheless, it's worth emphasizing that 99% of WPC's ABR is subject to contractual rent increases, out of which 53% is CPI-linked (20% is subject to some apps, while 33% is uncapped).

Let me say one more thing before we move to WPC's credit metrics. I very much like the fact that WPC is active in Europe, as European operating REITs are often capable of securing more attractive lease terms and initial cap rates. For instance, Realty Income secured higher initial cap rates for its European acquisitions than in the US. Please review the table below for details regarding O's acquisition activity in Q1-Q2 2024.

Not perfect, but a strong balance sheet

WPC has a strong balance sheet, scoring solidly with:

  • BBB+ credit rating
  • 4.4x fixed charge rate
  • Relatively short-term (4.4 years) but well-laddered debt maturity schedule

You can review the table above for a benchmark from some of WPC's competitors. WPC has negligible debt maturities in 2024 as the Company has already dealt with most of its 2024 debt maturities. Moreover, it has plenty of room above its key debt covenants.

Nevertheless, the Company will have meaningful debt maturing in 2025 and 2026, so should the current interest rate policy be upheld, WPC may face higher debt costs resulting from potential refinancing.

Investment Thesis and Risk Factors

Please remember that the thesis is concluded based on the assumption of abstracting from WPC's communication around its decision to exit the office property sector. Don't get me wrong - I believe the decision was right, but I didn't like the communication around it, and with that in mind, I declared WPC a 'hold' a while ago.

Due to requests from some readers, I decided to examine WPC solely from the business and valuation perspective, disregarding the above matter.

Under such an assumption, I believe WPC offers an attractive risk-to-reward ratio, especially after the recent REIT rally, which made WPC's valuation even more attractive (from a relative standpoint).

The Company showcases:

  • Safe financing structure with well-covered dividends at a reasonable forward-looking AFFO payout ratio of 74%
  • Top-tier business metrics are reflected in a high occupancy rate and WALT, as well as favorable rent escalators
  • Significant discount in terms of valuation, resulting in a substantial potential upside from the multiple expansion
  • Activity in Europe
  • Portfolio leaning towards an attractive property sector
  • Great diversification from different perspectives

As a result, investors who are not concerned about WPC's management's communication around the decision to exit office properties may regard WPC as a 'buy'. The Company has (almost) everything going for it regarding business and credit metrics, as well as a high margin of safety due to the low valuation, to constitute an attractive risk-to-reward ratio with double-digit upside potential.

When talking about risk factors, there are other matters to consider:

  • Uncertainty around the interest rate environment, especially given meaningful debt maturities in 2025-2026
  • The industrial property sector is facing some oversupply headwinds, which haven't yet posed a significant impact on WPC's business, but that may manifest a bit more in the next quarters
  • As WPC's properties are specific and mission-critical, it may struggle to find other tenants in the case of unrenewed lease expirations
  • With WPC's stock price beaten down, the Company has a relatively high cost of equity, negatively impacting its spreads on investments
  • Lack of knowledge regarding the unit-level reporting that WPC requires from its tenants and communication suggesting that the Company only pays closer attention once a given tenant starts to struggle

data.title: Fallen Angels: New Downgrades End Seven Month Drought
data.description:
The seven-month drought of fallen angels ended in September with two downgrades to high yield, while possible downgrades of Boeing (BA) and Paramount (PARA) have the potential to have a significant impact.

Fallen angels (as represented by the ICE US Fallen Angel High Yield 10% Constrained Index, “H0CF”) underperformed the broad high yield market (as represented by the ICE BofA US High Yield Index, “H0A0”) in September by 0.35% (1.28% vs. 1.63%), as lower quality bonds continued to outperform. This extended fallen angels’ Q3 underperformance to 0.23% (5.05% vs. 5.28%) and widened the YTD performance gap to 1.84% (6.18% vs. 8.03%). One of the key trends supporting high yield performance has been the outperformance of lower-rated bonds. The CCC & Lower-rated index has returned an impressive 15.35% YTD, compared to 7.19% for Single-B and 6.82% for BB-rated bonds. The higher quality exposure that is characteristic of fallen angels (87% BB vs 54% in the broad high yield market) has been a headwind in terms of relative performance. In September, the fallen angel index had two new entrants: OCI NV and VF Corp, which were downgraded to high yield, adding approximately $2.4bn in face value to the Index, while in early October, S&P placed Boeing on negative watch as approximately 33,000 workers remain on strike.

In mid-September, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates by 0.50%, bringing them to a range of 4.75%–5.00%. This marked the first rate cut in four years, reflecting progress toward the Fed’s 2% inflation target and indicating signs of softening in the labor market. While credit conditions have remained robust overall, the upcoming November elections and ongoing geopolitical risks in the Middle East could lead to heightened volatility in the coming months.

Fallen Angel Positioning for Q4 2024

Fallen angels offer a distinct value proposition within the high yield market. Since the pandemic, they have maintained higher quality, with over 80% of fallen angels rated BB, compared to approximately 50% for the broader high yield market. However, sector exposures have shifted significantly. Earlier this year, we highlighted Real Estate and Banking as key sectors to watch due to their overweight positions in fallen angels, offering attractive spreads and higher yields. These sectors have delivered positive performance, behind only Telecom, which was the highest contributor in both Q3 2024 and YTD.

Looking ahead to Q4, we continue to see Banking and Real Estate as sectors of interest, with overweights of 4.65% and 5.99%, while offering wider spreads (7bps and 110bps) and higher yields (48bps and 90bps), respectively. We are keeping a close eye on Capital Goods and Media, as downgrades of large issuers Boeing and Paramount have the potential to alter sector dynamics. Lastly, Retail and Telecom are significantly overweight in the fallen angel index by 15.53% and 6.24%, respectively.

Sector Allocations: Fallen Angel US High Yield vs. Broad US High Yield (As of 9/30/2024)

Fallen Angels Overall Statistics: Fallen angel yields continued their downward trend, ending the month at 6.43%. This marked the first time since the summer of 2022 that yields finished below 6.50%. The average price of fallen angels rose to $93.69, narrowing the gap relative to the long-term average, although remaining about $1.50 below that level. This reflected the broader trend in the high yield market, where average yield declined by 36bps to 6.98% and the average price increased to $96.72. We also note the shrinking yield differential between fallen angels and the broad high yield market. As of the end of September, the spread had narrowed to just 0.55%, its lowest level since the summer of 2021. This narrowing gap has occurred despite the stability of the overall credit quality of both markets, with the main driver being the compression of CCC and below spreads. The fallen angel index saw its largest market value increase in recent months, boosted by the addition of two fallen angels.

Fallen Angels: The seven month drought of fallen angles ended in September as OCI NV and VF Corporation were downgraded to high yield, adding approximately $2.4bn in face value to the index. OCI NV senior unsecured notes were downgraded to Ba1 from Baa3 by Moody’s as a result of multiple divestiture announcements by the company, which Moody’s believes will greatly diminish its scale and business profile. VF Corporation was also downgraded to Ba1 from Baa3 by Moody’s, reflecting a weaker credit profile as it transforms its business with various initiatives. We continue to watch for any action by Moody’s on Paramount following its current review, as well as any additional rating actions on Boeing following S&P placing it on negative watch. S&P stated that their action reflects the possibility of a downgrade if the strike continues towards the end of the year, as Boeing will likely incur a cash outflow of $10bn and require additional funding. Two downgrades are needed for Boeing to become a fallen angel eligible for index inclusion.

Rising Stars: Port of Newcastle Investments Financing was upgraded by S&P to BBB- from BB+ on stronger financials, joining Delta Air Lines as the only rising stars in Q3. It was downgraded to high yield exactly one year ago, September 2023, entering the fallen angel index at $82.79 and exiting at $95.59, providing a 15% price return.

Fallen Angels Performance by Sector: Basic Industry and Retail saw their exposure increase with the addition of OCI NV and VF Corporation. Basic Industry is now just shy of a 5% exposure while Retail jumped to close to 22% and continues to be the top sector exposure within fallen angels. Aside from these two sectors, there were no major shifts for Q3. In terms of spread, the Transportation sector saw 71bps of tightening, the largest of all sectors, while its price jumped by over 8%. Fallen angel spreads saw some volatility in Q3, as they reached 310bps in early August, but overall have been trending down so far this year. Real Estate spreads remain volatile and display the highest spreads of all sectors. September is the first month since February 2023, when the Banking sector was slightly above par (right before the mini-crisis) where various sectors have seen their price above par, but unfortunately this was not enough to outperform broad high yield. Regarding sector attribution vs broad high yield for Q3 and YTD, the lack of Media exposure, alongside limited exposure to Services and Basic Industry, have been detractors to performance while Telcom, Banking and Real Estate continue to be contributors.

Fallen Angels Performance by Rating: No significant changes in the fallen angels rating allocation for September, however, the Real Estate sector had two one-notch downgrades: Hudson Pacific Properties to BB3 from BB2 and Service Properties Trust to B3 from B2. In terms of performance vs broad high yield for September, Q3 and YTD, higher quality (BB) has positively contributed to performance but lower quality (Single-B and CCC & Below) have significantly detracted. The CCC & Below index is up 15.35% so far this year, with most of the performance coming in Q3.
